summary: Robert Langer recounts the severe rejection he faced early in his career when pioneering the intersection of engineering and biology. Despite having his first nine grants rejected, being denied faculty positions in chemical engineering, and being told by senior scientists that his drug delivery concepts were impossible, he persevered to build a highly successful career.

principles.statement: Put your head down and keep trying, regardless of what critics say.
principles.rationale: Groundbreaking interdisciplinary work often faces massive initial rejection from established experts who believe the concepts are impossible or outside your discipline.
principles.source_quote: you sort of put your head down and you keep trying uh regardless of what other people said

heuristics: Put your head down and keep trying regardless of what other people say.

signature_quotes.text: you sort of put your head down and you keep trying uh regardless of what other people said
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on how he survived the intense criticism and rejection of his early drug delivery concepts.

anti_patterns.description: Strictly enforcing academic silos, such as believing engineers have no place doing biological or oncology research.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It blinds experts to cross-disciplinary innovations. Early in his career, Langer's grants and job applications were rejected simply because reviewers felt an engineer should stick to math or energy, not biology.

anti_patterns.description: Assuming that because a molecule is large or requires organic solvents, it cannot be successfully delivered or will inevitably denature.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: This assumption caused early chemists and chemical engineers to completely dismiss Langer's pioneering drug delivery concepts as impossible.
